sections.heading: Molecular Pathogenesis Overview
sections.content: CUP exhibits complex molecular pathogenesis with multiple genetic alterations.

sections.heading: Chromosomal Abnormalities
sections.content: Chromosomal abnormalities include activation of angiogenesis genes (50%–89% of tumors), oncogene overexpression (10%–30%), epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition marker elevation (16%), and activation of hypoxia-related proteins (25%) and intracellular signaling molecules (20%–35%) [2].

sections.heading: Gene Overexpression
sections.content: Overexpression of several genes is commonly observed, including EGFR, MET, JAK3, BRAF, PIK3CA, c-kit/PDGFR (PDGFRα or PDGFRβ), RAS, BCL2, HER2, and TP53 [9,28,29,30].

sections.heading: Frequent Genetic Alterations in Study
sections.content: In a study of 252 patients, the most frequent genetic alterations were deletions in tumor suppressor genes TP53 (49.6%), CDKN2A (19.0%), and NOTCH1 (14.1%), and activation of oncogenes KRAS (23.4%), FGFR4 (14.9%), and PIK3CA (10.7%) [33].

sections.heading: Prognostic Implications
sections.content: KRAS activation and CDKN2A deletion were associated with poor prognosis.

sections.heading: BRD4-NUT Oncogene
sections.content: The BRD4-NUT oncogene, resulting from translocation t(15;19), is found in pediatric and young adult midline carcinomas [31,32].

sections.heading: Chromosomal Instability
sections.content: Chromosomal instability may contribute to aggressive behavior [2,34].

sections.heading: Therapeutic Targets
sections.content: These alterations provide potential targets for therapy, such as BRAF V600E, NTRK fusions, RET fusions, and HER2 amplification, guiding biomarker-driven treatments.
